steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence reads: "one of the nearly two thousand African ________ during the decade following the Civil War." The blank needs a plural noun (matching "two thousand") that can be modified by the relative clause "who won elections." No possessive is needed — the clause modifies who these people are, not something they own.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Americans' who won elections': Both apostrophes are wrong. Americans' is possessive, which makes no sense here, and elections' is also possessive for no reason. ✗
  • Choice B — Americans who won election's: election's is a singular possessive that doesn't fit — nothing is shown to belong to "election." ✗
  • Choice C — American's who won elections: American's is a singular possessive. The sentence needs a plural noun to match "two thousand," and no possessive is called for. ✗
  • Choice D — Americans who won elections: Plain plural noun, no apostrophe. The relative clause "who won elections" attaches correctly, and the plural matches "nearly two thousand." ✓
That matches Choice D — Americans who won elections.
